chapters.title: The dual nature of Hamilton
chapters.text:
What later became an acclaimed Broadway musical that would go on to tour the world and win 11 Tony Awards was at first an absolutely crazy idea. During his vacation in Mexico, Lin-Manuel Miranda caught an infectious inspiration while reading Ron Chernow’s biography of Alexander Hamilton — one of the United States founding fathers, an immigrant, and a desperate hot head. At first, Lin-Manuel Miranda was thinking of creating a hip-hop concept album about Hamilton’s life. Only much later, when it was already in the works, the ambitious idea to transform it into a musical appeared.

Hamilton is a story of two different revolutions — the 18th-century revolution, mirrored in the show’s libretto, and the musical and societal revolution that the show itself triggered. The story of the show’s creation is a wonderful and inspiring testimony to the power of ambition and human creativity.

The narrative of the show’s creation amplifies the show’s themes. ~ Jeremy McCarter

Lin-Manuel Miranda,

Even though Hamilton looks almost effortless when you see it on stage, its creation definitely wasn’t. It took approximately 1000 different choices for this musical to become what it is now.

In the following chapters, we’ll dive into the story of the musical and American revolutions: behind-the-scenes action, peculiarities of casting, and creating music, costumes, and decorations.

The story of Hamilton shows how the idea of a single person can transform hundreds of lives.

chapters.title: The intertwining of history and fiction
chapters.text:
When Lin-Manuel Miranda began to work on the lyrics of the first Hamilton songs, questions started pouring out of him, so he needed to know the smallest details of the founding father’s daily routine and habits. He sought out Ron Chernow, who was best equipped to answer due to his extensive knowledge about Hamilton’s life.

Apart from being an acclaimed writer, Pulitzer Prize winner, and historian, at heart, Ron Chernow was an avid fan of the founding father in question. He wrote many extensive biographies, including Alexander Hamilton’s and George Washington’s, and always wanted to see those inspiring stories played out on a stage or a movie set. Needless to say, he was more than happy to become a historical consultant during the show’s creation.

Of course, Lin-Manuel Miranda still had to invent many details by himself — even a professional like Chernow could not find out how 18th-century politicians liked their coffee. But Chernow had another vital role — he was a constant cheerleader during this challenging creative process. Knowing that this was quite a risky thing to do, he always made sure to provide the necessary encouragement.

When Ron Chernow first heard the actors sing through Act 1, he was indeed astonished — those who portrayed the founding fathers were Black and Latino, nothing like their historical counterparts. Lin-Manuel Miranda mentioned that this was a matter of absolute principle — Hamilton’s America has transformed, and the show had to portray that change.

This is a story about America then, told by America now. ~ Tommy Kail

Lin-Manuel Miranda,

What Ron Chernow saw on the stage that day deeply moved him. He then became an ardent defender of the idea that casting choices shouldn’t be determined by race, constantly monitoring the rehearsals from then on, providing historical insights and creative ideas. Sometimes Lin-Manuel Miranda had to argue for the liberties he had taken to ensure the coherent flow of the story. Chernow allowed them — he understood that this time around, Lin-Manuel Miranda was the one telling Hamilton’s story.

History is in part created by the person who tells the story.

chapters.title: Revolutionary decorations for the revolutionary show
chapters.text:
Hamilton was a natural New Yorker, and much like his favorite city, he was loud, boisterous, ambitious, and opinionated. So, New York as the show’s setting was an obvious and natural choice. Lin-Manuel Miranda even went as far as writing a new song that didn't necessarily fit the overall narrative just to squeeze in an R&B ode to the city. For him, New York was a place of possibilities and stark contrasts, the very essence of Hamilton’s story.

While the choice of location was an easy one to make, the process of creating the suitable backdrop and decorations was anything but. David Korins, the show’s principal designer, soon realized that he couldn’t depict the show’s many settings. He would have to create something that resembled all of them simultaneously — a common thread between the scenes that ignites imagination and is easy to manipulate. After weeks of thorough research, Korins came up with the central idea — a city not fully built yet, a place that immigrants constructed from the materials found on their ships.

The final result may not have looked like anything spectacular on its own but came truly alive when the show’s cast set foot on it for the first time. It didn’t look like modern New York or New York in 1776 but an idealized version of the city.

Hamilton created a perfect New York that we strive for, where people of all races, genders, and social backgrounds can sing and dance together freely.

Creating a fashion style that reflects the era of revolution wasn’t easy either. Paul Tazewell, the man who designed all the costumes, spent months fretting over the best possible fashion choice — he wanted to find the place where modern days and the days of revolution meet. Just like Korins, Tazewell researched every source that he could find but was still short of ideas. In the end, he decided to improvise and figure out the rest later. His first trial was a success; he put the actors into period clothes from the neck down and modern from the neck up. It was a bold and strong choice to put Black and Latino actors into the Continental Army’s uniform, but somehow, it worked.

One of the secrets of the show’s success is the diligence and profound professionalism of every person who contributed to the production. Settings, decorations, and costumes reinforced and strengthened the messages of Hamilton.

chapters.title: Hamilton is all about talent and hard work
chapters.text:
The show’s triumph wouldn’t be as huge if the casting choices were anything less than perfect. And, though it might seem like many of the choices were difficult to make, they really weren’t.

The first significant shoes that an actor had to fill were George Washington’s. It took a lot of guts for someone to undertake the role of a beloved father of the nation — just and complex at the same time. This burden (or a blessing!) was Chris Jackson’s to bear. He was Lin-Manuel Miranda’s good friend and talented colleague and was an obvious choice for the show’s creative team. Upon meeting Jackson, Ron Chernow, though apprehensive at first, saw the makings of a Washington in him that he couldn’t explain verbally.

After landing the role, Jackson poured everything into his research — he read every Washington biography he could get his hands on and consulted with Chernow and Lin-Manuel Miranda. This work ethic certainly paid off, and his performance on the opening night was powerful and captivating. The experience, he said, was extremely rewarding. It made him feel like a part of America instead of an afterthought.

The diverse cast of Hamilton highlighted the unacknowledged role of racial and ethnic minorities in building America.

Another spot-on choice was Renée Elise Goldsberry for the role of Angelica Schuyler. She almost missed out on the opportunity due to personal reasons, but the show’s team insisted she audition. Her task wasn’t an easy one. “Satisfied” is one of the fastest, most technically, and emotionally difficult songs in the show, and she had to learn it overnight! Despite her worries, Renée Elise Goldsberry was a perfect fit, and the team immediately fell in love with what she brought to the character.

One more casting choice worth mentioning is Aaron Burr — the narrator of the story, Hamilton’s equal opposite. The task of portraying him fell into the talented hands of Leslie Odom, Jr. — a young actor and rapper. Before Leslie, actors who tried to portray Burr were way too similar to Lin. The show needed contrast between Burr and Hamilton; where Hamilton was scruffy, hot-headed, and boisterous, Burr was calm, level-headed, and reserved. That is why Leslie Odom, Jr. was a great fit, and like Jackson, he threw himself into work, preparing himself both factually and emotionally.

Lin is asking you to bring your complete and total self to the stage — all your joy, all your rage, all your pain, your capacity for fun. ~ Leslie Odom, Jr.

Lin-Manuel Miranda,

The show’s casting, costumes, setting, and music made it a daring and unique project. But it doesn’t challenge history in vain — the show’s story presents an important lesson about the workings of time and history. History sets no limitations upon the possibilities that the future might bring.

The past does not define the future — even the unlikeliest changes can occur if there is hope.

chapters.title: The cast’s favorite audience were always children
chapters.text:
Hamilton first opened at the Public Theater in 2015 and immediately earned critical acclaim. The auditorium was filled to the brim every day, and the audience members were as diverse and inspired as the show’s cast. They all liked the spectacle, but the show’s true test came a bit later when the entire audience comprised teenagers from New York public high schools.

Perform a musical for one of those conventional audiences, and you know what to expect. Put it on for a couple hundred kids who have never set foot in a theater before, and there is no guessing. ~ Lin-Manuel Miranda

Lin-Manuel Miranda,

The cast and team were extremely worried about the performance, and so were the kids’ teachers. They spent weeks preceding the show talking with their students about the history of the American revolution and an unlikely hero — an immigrant founding father. Thankfully, the experience was wonderful and rewarding for both. The students liked it because the theater told them a story about themselves, one that they didn’t know yet.

Hamilton’s ability to influence, engage, and move in the deepest of ways is the show’s greatest strength.

For Daveed Diggs (Lafayette), the regular shows for students became the highlight of the Hamilton experience. The actors fed off the adolescent energy and gave as good as they got.

Later, students employed their experience and the show’s distinctive qualities in order to create their own hip-hop lyrics about the main issues of today’s America — gun control, same-sex marriage, drugs, and so on. All of this meant that the show’s true impact wasn’t on Broadway or in New York — it was in high schools across America.

chapters.title: The power of raw, unfiltered emotions
chapters.text:
Till his death, Alexander Hamilton was driven by his burning passion and ambition. The show has been built on the same principles and ideas. Its success is the result of a variety — cultural, motivational, historical, emotional.

The show’s true story is not the chronological and historical one. Hamilton is about a vivid and believable emotional journey.

Perhaps one of the best examples of the show’s emotional range is how it deals with loss and grief. Hamilton and his wife had to get over the most terrible loss — the loss of their child, Philip. For the longest time, Lin-Manuel Miranda couldn’t write a song that would fit such emotional intensity. But then “It’s quiet uptown” was born on his piano, and everyone — actors, staff members, and the band — wept.

This song was about loss and the limitation of words — both notions very familiar to Hamilton. He was renowned for his unique fluency and the masterful ways he wielded language. Yet his story shows that no matter how eloquent or intelligent a person is, there is always a limit to what can be verbally expressed. There always comes a moment when quiet becomes more powerful than anything you can find in the dictionary.

“It’s quiet uptown” was written to console the broken heart of a family that lived long ago and helps many of us dealing with similar pain now.

If art can help us grieve, can help us mourn, then lean on it. ~ Lin-Manuel Miranda

Lin-Manuel Miranda,

Did you know? There are nearly 24,000 words in Hamilton, which is significantly more than in Shakespeare’s The Merchant of Venice or Macbeth.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Alexander Hamilton’s life was full of contradictions, arguments, and struggles. He fought for the newly formed states in the Revolution, and he fought for his chance at a better life. He is the embodiment of America then and America now — an immigrant who took his chances and climbed to the top with the help of hard work and unique perseverance. Though he brought about many changes that, in the long run, aided the development of the strong democratic tradition in the United States, his legacy, before the creation of the show, was largely forgotten.

Thankfully, Alexander Hamilton’s story eventually fell into the right hands, and to this day, it continues to empower and inspire people around the world. His obsession with being remembered dictated every step along his way. Even now, Hamilton invites us to think about the very nature of legacies. What will we leave behind? Who will one day tell our story?

Hamilton: An American Musical is a truly monumental piece of art. For it to become a reality, a beautiful show that people everywhere can see performed on stage, hundreds of professionals had to give it their best.

There are many important messages within the musical’s libretto, but the show’s central point is the idea that people who have a vision and a set of ideals can transform the world. And so can words and stories, if told at the right place at the right time.

We tell stories of people who are gone because, like any powerful stories, they have the potential to inspire and to change the world. ~ Jeremy McCarter

Lin-Manuel Miranda,

Try this

Find a way to visualize your ideas and return to them from time to time — this will be the first step towards making them a reality. If you can, watch Hamilton and make some personal conclusions — everyone can learn something different.

Read it till the end?
